grammarLessons.name: المستقبل — Future Tense with سـ / سوف

cards.grammarNote:
Arabic profession nouns are masculine by default. Feminine is formed by adding **ة** (ta marbuta):
**طبيب** → **طبيبة** (tabib → tabiba = female doctor)
**مهندس** → **مهندسة** (muhandis → muhandisa = female engineer)
Unlike Spanish, no article is dropped: **أنا طبيب** (ana tabib) = I am a doctor.

**مُمَرِّض** (mumarriḍ, male nurse) / **مُمَرِّضة** (mumarriḍa, female nurse). From the root **م-ر-ض** (m-r-ḍ, illness/nursing). The **مُفَعِّل** (mufaʿʿil) pattern indicates someone who performs a repeated action — here, caring for the sick.
Broken plural: **مُمَرِّضون** (mumarriḍūn, male nurses), **مُمَرِّضات** (mumarriḍāt, female nurses).

**مُهَندِس** (muhandis) comes from the root **ه-ن-د-س** (h-n-d-s), borrowed from the Persian *handāzah* (measurement/geometry) via medieval Arabic. The **مُفَنعِل** pattern marks a practitioner.
Feminine: **مُهَندِسة** (muhandisa). Plural: **مُهَندِسون** (muhandisūn, m.) / **مُهَندِسات** (muhandisāt, f.).

**مُبَرمِج** (mubarmmij) from root **ب-ر-م-ج** (b-r-m-j) — a modern quadriliteral root derived from **برنامج** (barnāmaj, programme). The **مُفَعلِل** pattern on quadriliterals marks the agent/practitioner.
**أعمل كـ** (aʿmal ka-) = I work as. The prefix **كـ** (ka-) = as/like, attached directly to the following noun.

**طَاهٍ** (ṭāhin) is the active participle (**فَاعِل**) of the root **ط-ه-ي** (ṭ-h-y, to cook). The final ي drops before case endings, giving the tanwin form **طَاهٍ** (indefinite) or **الطَّاهِي** (definite).
Related: **مَطبَخ** (maṭbakh, kitchen) follows the **مَفعَل** place-pattern from root **ط-ب-خ** (ṭ-b-kh, to cook).
cards.englishPhrase: The cook works in the kitchen.

cards.grammarNote:
**نَادِل** (nādil, waiter) / **نَادِلة** (nādila, waitress). The **فَاعِل** pattern marks the doer — here, one who serves/calls.
**قَائِمة الطَّعَام** (qāʾimat aṭ-ṭaʿām) = menu, literally 'list of food' — an **إضافة** (iḍāfa) possessive chain. The definite article only appears on the second noun.

**مُدِير** (mudīr) from root **د-و-ر** (d-w-r, to turn/revolve/manage). Pattern **مُفعِل** = one who causes something. Feminine: **مُدِيرة** (mudīra). Plural: **مُدَراء** (mudarāʾ, broken plural) or **مُدِيرون** (mudīrūn).
Related: **إِدارة** (idāra, administration/management) from the same root.

**طَالِب** (ṭālib) = student, literally 'one who seeks/requests.' **فَاعِل** active participle from root **ط-ل-ب** (ṭ-l-b, to seek/request/demand). The same root gives **طَلَب** (ṭalab, request) and **مَطلوب** (maṭlūb, required/wanted).
Feminine: **طَالِبة** (ṭāliba). Plural: **طُلَّاب** (ṭullāb) or **طَالِبون** (ṭālibūn).

**مُهَندِس مِعمَارِي** = engineer + adjectival form of **عِمَارة** (ʿimāra, architecture/building). Root **ع-م-ر** (ʿ-m-r, to build/inhabit) also gives **عُمر** (ʿumr, lifespan) and **عَمَّار** (ʿammār, builder).
**مِعمَارِي** is the adjective: معماري literally means 'of construction/architecture.'

**مُحَامٍ** (muḥāmin) from root **ح-م-ي/و** (ḥ-m-y, to protect/defend). **مُفَاعِل** pattern = one who actively defends. Indefinite form ends in ـنٍ (the ي drops before case endings).
Feminine: **مُحَامِيَة** (muḥāmiya). The note **أختي** (ukhtī) = my sister — possessive suffix **ي** (-ī) attaches to the noun.

**كُرَة القَدَم** (kurat al-qadam) = ball of the foot — an **إضافة** (iḍāfa) possessive chain. **كُرة** (kura, ball) + **القَدَم** (al-qadam, the foot). Root **ق-د-م** (q-d-m) also means 'front/ahead': **قَدِيم** (qadīm, old/ancient), **مُقَدِّمة** (muqaddima, introduction).
**شَعبِيَّة** (shaʿbiyya, popular) from root **ش-ع-ب** (sh-ʿ-b, people/folk).
cards.englishPhrase: Soccer is popular all over the world.
cards.phrase: كرة القدم شعبية في جميع أنحاء العالم.
cards.phraseRomanized: kurat al-qadam shaʿbīya fī jamīʿ anḥāʾ al-ʿālam.

cards.grammarNote:
**مُتَقَاعِد** (mutaqāʿid, retired) — Form VI active participle from root **ق-ع-د** (q-ʿ-d, to sit/stay behind). Form VI (**تَفَاعَلَ**) here implies withdrawal: **قَعَدَ** (qaʿada, to sit/remain) → **تَقَاعَدَ** (taqāʿada, to retire). **التَّقَاعُد** (at-taqāʿud) = retirement — verbal noun.
Feminine: **مُتَقَاعِدة** (mutaqāʿida). Used as both noun and adjective: **هو متقاعد** = he is retired.

**القِرَاءَة** (al-qirāʾa, reading) is a verbal noun (مَصدَر, maṣdar) from root **ق-ر-أ** (q-r-ʾ, to read). The same root gives **قُرآن** (Qurʾān, the recitation) and **قَارِئ** (qāriʾ, reader — **فَاعِل** pattern).
Verbal nouns in Arabic function like gerunds: أُحِبُّ القِراءَة = I love reading (the reading).

**الكِتَابَة** (al-kitāba, writing) — verbal noun (مَصدَر) from root **ك-ت-ب** (k-t-b, to write). This root is one of the most productive in Arabic: **كِتَاب** (kitāb, book), **كَاتِب** (kātib, writer — **فَاعِل**), **مَكتَب** (maktab, office/desk — **مَفعَل** place pattern), **مَكتوب** (maktūb, written/letter).

**الرَّسم** (ar-rasm) = drawing/painting/sketching. From root **ر-س-م** (r-s-m, to draw/sketch). **رَسَّام** (rassām, artist/painter — intensive **فَعَّال** pattern). **رُسُوم** (rusūm, drawings, broken plural).
**الرَّسم** is a sun-letter word: **الـ** assimilates to **ر**, so it is pronounced *ar-rasm*, not *al-rasm*.

**الرَّسم** (ar-rasm) serves as both 'drawing' and 'painting' in Arabic — the same verbal noun covers both. From root **ر-س-م** (r-s-m, to draw/sketch/inscribe). The sun letter **ر** assimilates the definite article: *al-rasm* → *ar-rasm*.
Distinguish **رَسم** (rasm, drawing/sketch) from **لَوحَة** (lawḥa, a painted artwork/canvas).

**التَّصوِير** (at-taṣwīr, photography/imaging) from root **ص-و-ر** (ṣ-w-r, to picture/portray). **تَفعِيل** verbal noun pattern = a Form II action. **صُورة** (ṣūra, photo/image, pl. **صُوَر** — broken plural), **مُصَوِّر** (muṣawwir, photographer — active participle Form II).
**هِوَايَة** (hiwāya, hobby) is of uncertain origin; the possessive suffix **ي** (-ī) = my.

**المُوسِيقَى** (al-mūsīqā) is a loanword from Greek *mousikē* (the art of the Muses), entering Arabic via Greek and Syriac. It is one of many scientific/artistic terms borrowed into Arabic during the Abbasid translation movement (8th–10th c.).
**أَستَمِع إلى** (astamīʿ ilā) = I listen to. Root **س-م-ع** (s-m-ʿ, to hear); Form X **اِستَمَعَ** = to listen attentively.
cards.englishPhrase: I listen to music every day.

cards.grammarNote:
**الغِنَاء** (al-ghinā', singing) — verbal noun from root **غ-ن-ي** (gh-n-y, to sing/be self-sufficient). **غَنَّى** (ghannā, to sing — Form II intensive), **مُغَنٍّ** (mughanin, singer — Form II active participle, drops ي before case endings).
**أُغنِيَة** (ughniya, song) from the same root; plural **أَغَانٍ** (aghānin — broken plural). Note the moon letter **غ**: the article stays *al-ghinā'*, not assimilated.

**العَزف** (al-ʿazf, playing an instrument) from root **ع-ز-ف** (ʿ-z-f, to play a musical instrument). **عَزَفَ على** (ʿazafa ʿalā) = to play [instrument]. **آلة مُوسِيقِيَّة** (āla mūsīqiyya) = musical instrument — **آلة** (āla, tool/instrument) + **مُوسِيقِيَّة** (nisba adjective).
**الغِيتَار** (al-ghītār) is a loanword from Spanish *guitarra* / English 'guitar'.

**الرَّقص** (ar-raqṣ, dancing) from root **ر-ق-ص** (r-q-ṣ, to dance/jump). Sun letter **ر** causes assimilation: *ar-raqṣ*.
**رَاقِص** (rāqiṣ, dancer — **فَاعِل**), **رَاقِصة** (rāqiṣa, female dancer). Plural of the verbal noun is uncommon; you say **رُقصَة** (raqṣa) for a single dance.

**الطَّبخ** (aṭ-ṭabkh, cooking) from root **ط-ب-خ** (ṭ-b-kh, to cook). Sun letter **ط** causes assimilation.
**طَبَخَ** (ṭabakha, to cook), **طَاهٍ** (ṭāhin, cook — **فَاعِل**), **مَطبَخ** (maṭbakh, kitchen — **مَفعَل** place pattern).
**أستمتع بـ** (astamtiʿ bi-) = I enjoy. Root **م-ت-ع** (m-t-ʿ, pleasure), Form X with **بـ** preposition.

**البُستَنة** (al-bustana, gardening) from **بُستَان** (bustān, garden/orchard) — a Persian loanword (*bustān* = place of fragrance). The ending **ـة** (ta marbuta) converts the noun to a verbal activity noun.
**مُرِيحة** (murīḥa, relaxing, feminine) — Form IV active participle of root **ر-و-ح** (r-w-ḥ, to rest/comfort). It agrees with **البُستَنة** (feminine).

**المَشي** (al-mashī, walking) — verbal noun from root **م-ش-ي** (m-sh-y, to walk). **لِمَسَافَات طَوِيلة** (li-masāfāt ṭawīla) = for long distances. **مَسَافة** (masāfa, distance) from root **س-و-ف** (s-w-f), plural **مَسَافات** (sound feminine plural).
**الجِبَال** (al-jibāl, mountains) is the broken plural of **جَبَل** (jabal, mountain) — pattern **فِعَال**.
cards.englishPhrase: I like hiking in the mountains.

cards.grammarNote:
**الجَري** (al-jarī, running) — verbal noun from root **ج-ر-ي** (j-r-y, to run/flow). Related: **مَجرَى** (majrā, course/channel — **مَفعَل** pattern), **جَارٍ** (jārin, running/flowing — **فَاعِل**). The phrase uses **أَركُض** (arkuḍ, I run) from root **ر-ك-ض** (r-k-ḍ) — a near-synonym for running.
Both roots are used: **جَرَى** (to run/flow, often metaphorical), **رَكَضَ** (to run, physical movement).

**الهَروَلة** (al-harwala, jogging/trotting) from root **ه-ر-و-ل** (h-r-w-l) — a quadriliteral root. **هَروَلَ** (harwala, to jog/trot) is the Form I of this four-letter root. Quadriliteral verbs have their own pattern: **فَعلَلَ** (faʿlala). **يُهَروِل** (yuharwil) = he jogs.
Distinct from **الجَري** (running at full speed) — هرولة implies a moderate pace.

**رُكُوب الدَّرَّاجة** (rukūb ad-darrāja) = riding the bicycle — an **إضافة** (iḍāfa) possessive chain: verbal noun + definite noun. **رُكُوب** (rukūb, riding) — verbal noun from root **ر-ك-ب** (r-k-b, to ride/mount). **رَاكِب** (rākib, rider — **فَاعِل**), **مَركَبة** (markaba, vehicle).
**الدَّرَّاجة** (ad-darrāja, bicycle) from root **د-ر-ج** (d-r-j, to go in steps/roll) — sun letter **د** causes assimilation.
cards.phrase: أركب الدراجة في عطلة نهاية الأسبوع.

cards.grammarNote:
**السِّبَاحة** (as-sibāḥa, swimming) from root **س-ب-ح** (s-b-ḥ, to swim/glorify God). The same root gives **سُبحَانَ الله** (subḥāna allāh, 'glory be to God') — a beautiful polysemy. **سَبَّاح** (sabbāḥ, swimmer — intensive **فَعَّال** pattern).
**السِّبَاحة** is a sun-letter word: pronounced *as-sibāḥa*.

**اليُوغَا** (al-yūghā) is a loanword from Sanskrit *yoga* (union/discipline), entering Arabic as a modern borrowed term. Loanwords in Arabic usually take the definite article **الـ** like any other noun.
**تُمَارِس** (tumāris, she practices) from root **م-ر-س** (m-r-s, to practice) — Form III verb; **مُمَارَسة** (mumārasa, practice — verbal noun Form III).

**التَّمرِين** (at-tamrīn, exercise/training) from root **م-ر-ن** (m-r-n, to be supple/practise). Form II verbal noun pattern **تَفعِيل**.
**الصَّالة الرِّيَاضِيَّة** (aṣ-ṣāla ar-riyāḍiyya, sports hall/gym) — **رِيَاضة** (riyāḍa, sport) from root **ر-و-ض** (r-w-ḍ, to tame/cultivate). **رِيَاضِيَّة** is the adjectival form (nisba adjective with **ـِيَّة** suffix).
cards.englishPhrase: I go to the gym for exercise.

cards.grammarNote:
**السَّفَر** (as-safar, travel) from root **س-ف-ر** (s-f-r, to travel/reveal). Related: **سَفِير** (safīr, ambassador — literally 'one who travels/mediates'), **سِفَارة** (sifāra, embassy). The English word 'safari' (through Swahili) traces back to this same Arabic root.
**أُحِبُّ السَّفَر** — a definite verbal noun used as a direct object.

**التَّخيِيم** (at-takhyīm, camping) — Form II verbal noun from root **خ-ي-م** (kh-y-m, to pitch a tent/encamp). **خَيمَة** (khayma, tent — from the same root), **مُخَيَّم** (mukhayam, camp/refugee camp — Form II passive participle).
The sun letter **ت** causes assimilation: **التخييم** → pronounced *at-takhyīm*. **الصَّيف** (aṣ-ṣayf, summer) from root **ص-ي-ف** (ṣ-y-f).
cards.englishPhrase: We go camping in the summer.

cards.grammarNote:
**أَلعَاب الفِيدِيو** (alʿāb al-fīdyū) = video games. **أَلعَاب** is the broken plural of **لُعبَة** (luʿba, game) — pattern **أَفعَال**. Root **ل-ع-ب** (l-ʿ-b, to play): **يَلعَب** (yalʿab, he plays), **لَاعِب** (lāʿib, player — **فَاعِل**).
**الفِيدِيو** is a loanword from English 'video'.
cards.englishPhrase: He plays video games at night.
cards.phrase: هو يلعب ألعاب الفيديو في الليل.
cards.phraseRomanized: huwa yal'ab al'ab al-vidyu fi al-layl.

cards.grammarNote:
**مُشَاهَدة** (mushāhada, watching) — Form III verbal noun from root **ش-ه-د** (sh-h-d, to witness/see). Form III implies reciprocity: **شَاهَدَ** (shāhada, to watch/view). Related: **شَاهِد** (shāhid, witness), **مَشهَد** (mashhad, scene/sight — place noun).
**التِّلفَاز** (at-tilfāz, television) — loanword from Latin/Greek via French *télévision*, Arabized. Sun letter **ت** causes assimilation. Colloquial: **تلفزيون** (tilfiziyūn).

**الاِستِمَاع** (al-istimāʿ, listening) — Form X verbal noun from root **س-م-ع** (s-m-ʿ, to hear). Form X (**اِستَفعَلَ**) adds intention/effort: **سَمِعَ** (samiʿa, to hear passively) vs. **اِستَمَعَ** (istamaʿa, to listen actively/attentively). Always followed by **إلى** (ilā, to): **اِستَمَعَ إلى** = to listen to.
Compare: **سَمِعَ** (heard) — passive; **اِستَمَعَ** (listened) — deliberate.

**الحِرَف اليَدَوِيَّة** (al-ḥiraf al-yadawiyya) = handcrafts/manual crafts. **حِرَف** (ḥiraf) is the broken plural of **حِرفَة** (ḥirfa, craft/trade/profession) from root **ح-ر-ف** (ḥ-r-f, to deviate/craft). Pattern **فِعَل** (fiʿal).
**يَدَوِيَّة** (yadawiyya) = manual/hand-made — nisba adjective from **يَد** (yad, hand) + **ـِيَّة** suffix. The phrase is a definite noun + definite adjective agreement.

**الشِّطرَنج** (ash-shiṭranj, chess) — a loanword from Sanskrit *caturanga* ('four divisions of an army') via Persian *shatranj*. Arabic adopted it during the Abbasid era when chess spread westward from India. The sun letter **ش** assimilates the article: *ash-shiṭranj*.
**يَلعَب الشِّطرَنج** = to play chess — note Arabic uses **يَلعَب** (to play) directly with the game name, unlike **يَعزِف على** (to play an instrument).

**أَلعَاب لَوحِيَّة** (alʿāb lawḥiyya) = board games. **أَلعَاب** is the broken plural of **لُعبَة** (luʿba, game) — pattern **أَفعَال**. **لَوحِيَّة** (lawḥiyya) = of a board/tablet — nisba adjective from **لَوح** (lawḥ, board/plank/tablet). The same word now means 'tablet' (electronic): **اللَّوح الإِلِكترُوني** = iPad/tablet.
Root **ل-ع-ب** (l-ʿ-b): **لَعِبَ** (to play), **لَاعِب** (player), **مَلعَب** (playground).
cards.englishPhrase: We play board games on weekends.
cards.phrase: نلعب الألعاب اللوحية في عطلة نهاية الأسبوع.
cards.phraseRomanized: nal'ab al-al'ab al-lawhiyya fi 'utlat nihayat al-usbu'.

cards.grammarNote:
**يَعمَل** (yaʿmal, he works) from root **ع-م-ل** (ʿ-m-l, work/do) — one of the most productive roots in Arabic: **عَمَل** (ʿamal, work), **عَامِل** (ʿāmil, worker — **فَاعِل**), **مَعمَل** (maʿmal, laboratory/workshop — **مَفعَل** place pattern), **عَمَلِيَّة** (ʿamaliyya, operation/process).
**مُستَشفَى** (mustashfā, hospital) from root **ش-ف-ي** (sh-f-y, to heal): **شِفَاء** (shifāʾ, cure), **Form X** = place where healing is sought.

**يَتَقَاضَى** (yataqāḍā, he earns/collects a salary) — Form VI of root **ق-ض-ي** (q-ḍ-y, to judge/fulfil/collect a debt). Form VI implies mutual action or gradual fulfilment: **قَضَى** (qaḍā, to fulfil/spend time) → **تَقَاضَى** (taqāḍā, to receive/collect what is owed).
**رَاتِباً جَيِّداً** — both words end in **ـاً** (tanwin accusative): **رَاتِب** (salary) as direct object + **جَيِّد** (good) as adjective, both indefinite accusative.

**يَتَقَاعَد** (yataqāʿad, he retires) — Form VI of root **ق-ع-د** (q-ʿ-d, to sit/stay). Form VI implies mutual withdrawal or gradual stepping back: **قَعَدَ** (qaʿada, to sit/remain) → **تَقَاعَدَ** (taqāʿada, to retire/withdraw). **التَّقَاعُد** (at-taqāʿud) = retirement (verbal noun).
**العَام المَاضِي** = last year — **مَاضٍ** (māḍin, past/last) from root **م-ض-ي** (m-ḍ-y, to pass).

**وَظِيفة** (waẓīfa, job/function/duty) from root **و-ظ-ف** (w-ẓ-f, to assign a task). **فَعِيلة** pattern. **وَظَّفَ** (waẓẓafa, to employ — Form II), **مُوَظَّف** (muwaẓẓaf, employee — Form II passive participle). Plural: **وَظَائِف** (waẓāʾif — broken plural **فَعَائِل**).
Note: **وَظِيفة** (waẓīfa) = formal job/post, while **عَمَل** (ʿamal) = work in general. Use **وَظِيفة** for an official position.

**عَاطِل عَن العَمَل** (ʿāṭil ʿan al-ʿamal) = idle/away from work. **عَاطِل** (ʿāṭil, idle — **فَاعِل**) from root **ع-ط-ل** (ʿ-ṭ-l, to be idle/unemployed). The preposition **عَن** (ʿan, away from) completes the meaning. Literally 'one who is idle from work.'
Modern alternative: **بِدُون عَمَل** (bidūn ʿamal) = without work. Colloquially: **عَاطِل** alone is widely understood.

**يَدرُس** (yadrus, he studies) from root **د-ر-س** (d-r-s, to study/tread a path). Related: **دَرس** (dars, lesson), **دِرَاسة** (dirāsa, study/studies — verbal noun Form I), **مَدرَسة** (madrasa, school — **مَفعَلة** place pattern), **مُدَرِّس** (mudarris, teacher — Form II active participle).

**يَتَعَلَّم** (yataʿallam, he learns) — Form V of root **ع-ل-م** (ʿ-l-m, knowledge). Form V (**تَفَعَّلَ**) of Form II often means 'to acquire' the state: **عَلَّمَ** (ʿallama, to teach) → **تَعَلَّمَ** (taʿallama, to learn). Same root: **عِلم** (ʿilm, science/knowledge), **عَالِم** (ʿālim, scholar), **مَعلُومة** (maʿlūma, information), **عَالَم** (ʿālam, world).

**يَتَدَرَّب** (yatadarrab, he practices/trains) — Form V of root **د-ر-ب** (d-r-b, to train/accustom). **دَرَّبَ** (darraba, to train sb.) → **تَدَرَّبَ** (tadarraba, to train oneself/practice). Related: **تَدرِيب** (tadrīb, training), **مُدَرِّب** (mudarrib, trainer/coach).

**يُدَرِّس** (yudarris, he teaches) — Form II of root **د-ر-س** (d-r-s, to study). Form II doubles the middle radical and usually gives a causative or intensive meaning: **دَرَسَ** (darasa, to study) → **دَرَّسَ** (darrasa, to teach). Compare: **كَتَبَ** (to write) → **كَتَّبَ** (to make write), **عَلِمَ** (to know) → **عَلَّمَ** (to teach).

**يَبنِي** (yabnī, he builds) from root **ب-ن-ي** (b-n-y, to build). Related: **بِنَاء** (binā', building/construction), **مَبنَى** (mabnā, building/structure — **مَفعَل** pattern), **بَنِيَّة** (baniyya, structure).
**مَدرَسة** (madrasa, school) is a **مَفعَلة** place-noun from **د-ر-س** (to study) — where studying happens.
cards.englishPhrase: They are building a new school.

cards.grammarNote:
**يُصلِح** (yuṣliḥ, he fixes) — Form IV of root **ص-ل-ح** (ṣ-l-ḥ, to be good/sound). Form IV (**أَفعَلَ**) = causative: **صَلَحَ** (ṣalaḥa, to be sound/good) → **أَصلَحَ** (aṣlaḥa, to fix/reform). **إِصلَاح** (iṣlāḥ, repair/reform — Form IV verbal noun), **إِصلَاحَات** (iṣlāḥāt, reforms).
Related: **مُصلِح** (muṣliḥ, reformer), **صُلح** (ṣulḥ, peace/reconciliation).

**يَصنَع** (yaṣnaʿ, he makes/manufactures) from root **ص-ن-ع** (ṣ-n-ʿ, to make). **صَنَاعة** (ṣanāʿa, industry/craft), **صَانِع** (ṣāniʿ, maker — **فَاعِل**), **مَصنَع** (maṣnaʿ, factory — **مَفعَل** place pattern).
**القَهوَة** (al-qahwa, coffee) — a word Arabic gave to the world: English 'coffee', Turkish *kahve*, French *café* all come from this Arabic source.

**يُصَمِّم** (yuṣammim, he designs) — Form II of root **ص-م-م** (ṣ-m-m, to be firm/decide/design). **تَصمِيم** (taṣmīm, design — Form II verbal noun), **مُصَمِّم** (muṣammim, designer — Form II active participle).
**مَوَاقِع** (mawāqiʿ) is the broken plural of **مَوقِع** (mawqiʿ, site/location) — pattern **مَفَاعِل**.

**يَبِيع** (yabīʿ, he sells) from root **ب-ي-ع** (b-y-ʿ, to sell/trade). **بَيع** (bayʿ, sale), **بَائِع** (bāʾiʿ, seller — **فَاعِل**), **مَبِيع** (mabīʿ, sold item — **مَفعُول** pattern).
**السَّيَّارَات** (as-sayyārāt) = cars, sound feminine plural of **سَيَّارة** (sayyāra) — from root **س-ي-ر** (s-y-r, to travel).

**يَشتَرِي** (yashtarī, he buys) — Form VIII of root **ش-ر-ي** (sh-r-y, to buy/sell). Form VIII (**اِفتَعَلَ**) here adds a nuance of mutual exchange. **شِرَاء** (shirāʾ, purchase — verbal noun), **مُشتَرٍ** (mushtarin, buyer).
**حَاسُوب** (ḥāsūb, computer) from root **ح-س-ب** (ḥ-s-b, to calculate) — literally 'that which calculates.'
cards.englishPhrase: I need to buy a new computer.

cards.grammarNote: **يُسَاعِد** (yusāʿid, he helps) — Form III of root **س-ع-د** (s-ʿ-d, to be happy/assist). Form III (**فَاعَلَ**) often implies reciprocity or helping toward a goal: **سَعَدَ** (saʿada, to be happy) → **سَاعَدَ** (sāʿada, to assist). **مُسَاعَدة** (musāʿada, help/assistance — Form III verbal noun).

**يُدِير** (yudīr, he manages/directs) — Form IV of root **د-و-ر** (d-w-r, to turn/revolve). Form IV: **أَدَارَ** (adāra, to manage/run). **إِدَارة** (idāra, management/administration — Form IV verbal noun), **مُدِير** (mudīr, manager — Form IV active participle).
The root conveys rotation/running: **دَوْرة** (dawra, cycle/turn), **دَوَام** (dawām, continuity/permanence at work).

**يَجتَمِع** (yajtamiʿ, he meets/gathers) — Form VIII of root **ج-م-ع** (j-m-ʿ, to gather). Form VIII (**اِفتَعَلَ**) is reflexive: **جَمَعَ** (jamaʿa, to gather people) → **اِجتَمَعَ** (ijtamaʿa, to gather oneself/meet together). **مُجتَمَع** (mujtamaʿ, society — the place of gathering).
Note: **قَابَلَ** (qābala, Form III) = to meet one-on-one; **اِجتَمَعَ** = to meet as a group.

**يَبدَأ** (yabdaʾ, he starts) from root **ب-د-أ** (b-d-ʾ, to begin). **بِدَايَة** (bidāya, beginning), **مَبدَأ** (mabdaʾ, principle — **مَفعَل** pattern, literally 'the starting point').
**في الثَّامِنة** (fi ath-thāmina) = at eight (o'clock). Arabic uses the feminine ordinal: **الثَّامِنة** = the eighth (hour). The definite article assimilates: **الث** → pronounced *ath-thāmina* (sun letter ث).

**يُنهِي** (yunhī, he finishes) — Form IV of root **ن-ه-ي** (n-h-y, to end/forbid). Form IV (**أَفعَلَ**) = causative: **نَهَى** (nahā, to forbid/come to end) → **أَنهَى** (anhā, to finish/conclude). Related: **نِهَاية** (nihāya, end/conclusion), **مُنتَهٍ** (muntahin, finished — Form VIII active participle).

**يحب** (yuhibb) = to love / like — the subject is always the person, not the thing liked.
**أحب القراءة** (uhibb al-qira'a) = I like reading.
**هي تحب الغناء** (hiya tuhibb al-ghina') = she likes singing.
cards.englishPhrase: I like reading. She likes singing.

cards.grammarNote: **يَستَرخِي** (yastarkhi, he relaxes) — Form X of root **ر-خ-و/ي** (r-kh-w, to be loose/relaxed). Form X (**اِستَفعَلَ**) = to become or seek a state: **رَخَا** (rakhā, to be loose) → **اِستَرخَى** (istarkha, to relax/become loose). **اِسترَاحة** (istirāḥa, rest/break) is related from the same Form X idea.

**يَقضِي وَقتاً** (yaqḍī waqtan, he spends time) from root **ق-ض-ي** (q-ḍ-y, to fulfil/spend/pass). **قَضَى** (qaḍā) covers: to spend time, to fulfil an obligation, to rule/judge, to die. A remarkably rich root: **قَاضٍ** (qāḍin, judge — **فَاعِل**), **قَضَاء** (qaḍāʾ, judiciary/fate).
**وَقتاً كَثِيراً** — both in the accusative indefinite form (tanwin **ـاً**) as object + adjective.
cards.englishPhrase: I spend a lot of time reading.

cards.grammarNote:
**مُبدِع** (mubdiʿ, creative) — Form IV active participle of root **ب-د-ع** (b-d-ʿ, to create/innovate). **أَبدَعَ** (abdaʿa, to create/excel), **إِبدَاع** (ibdāʿ, creativity/innovation — Form IV verbal noun), **بَدِيع** (badīʿ, wondrous/original — adjective, also a name).
Feminine: **مُبدِعَة** (mubdiʿa). The **جِداً** (jiddan, very) comes after the predicate adjective.

**مُثِير لِلاِهتِمَام** (muthīr lil-ihtimām) = interesting, literally 'arousing/exciting for attention.' **مُثِير** (muthīr, arousing — Form IV active participle of **ث-و-ر**, to stir up). **اِهتِمَام** (ihtimām, attention/interest — Form VIII verbal noun from root **ه-م-م**, to concern/care).
This is a very common compound adjective in Modern Standard Arabic.

**مُجهِد** (mujhid, exhausting/stressful) — Form IV active participle of root **ج-ه-د** (j-h-d, to exert effort/strive). **جَهد** (jahd, effort), **جِهَاد** (jihād, striving/struggle), **مَجهُود** (majhūd, exerted effort). The pattern **مُفعِل** marks 'the one causing' the root action — so **مُجهِد** = 'that which causes exhaustion.'

**مُمِلّ** (mumill, boring/tedious) — Form IV active participle of root **م-ل-ل** (m-l-l, to be bored/weary). **مَلَل** (malal, boredom), **مَلَّ** (malla, to be bored). Pattern **مُفعِل** = 'that which causes' the state: **مُمِلّ** = 'that which causes boredom.'
**كَانَ مُمِلاً** = was boring: **كَانَ** (kāna, was) + adjective in the accusative (مُمِلاً with tanwin **ـاً**).

**رَاتِب** (rātib, salary) from root **ر-ت-ب** (r-t-b, to arrange/set in order). Literally 'something arranged/fixed regularly.' **رَتَّبَ** (rattaba, to arrange), **تَرتِيب** (tartīb, arrangement/order).
Phrasing: **ما هو الرَّاتِب؟** = What is the salary? Arabic question with **ما** (mā, what) + **هو** (huwa, it is) + definite noun.

**جَدوَل** (jadwal, schedule/table/river channel) — an interesting word with multiple meanings: originally 'a small stream,' then extended to 'a column/table/schedule.' Borrowed from Persian. Plural: **جَدَاوِل** (jadāwil — broken plural, pattern **فَعَاوِل**).
**مَرِن** (marin, flexible) from root **م-ر-ن** (m-r-n, to be supple/train).

**إِجَازة** (ijāza, vacation/permission/leave) — Form IV verbal noun from root **ج-و-ز** (j-w-z, to pass/allow). Originally means 'permission/authorization.' In Modern Arabic it covers both official leave and holiday travel.
**الأُسبُوع القَادِم** (al-usbūʿ al-qādim) = next week. **القَادِم** (qādim, coming/next) — **فَاعِل** of **ق-د-م** (q-d-m, to come/arrive).
cards.englishPhrase: I'm going on vacation next week.

cards.grammarNote:
**اِسترَاحة** (istirāḥa, rest/break) — Form X verbal noun from root **ر-و-ح** (r-w-ḥ, to breathe/rest). Form X (**اِستَفعَلَ**) = to seek rest: **رَاحَ** (rāḥa, to rest/leave) → **اِسترَاحَ** (istirāḥa, to take a rest). Related: **رَاحة** (rāḥa, comfort), **رُوح** (rūḥ, soul/spirit — literally 'breath').
**لِنَأخُذ** (li-naʾkhudh) = let's take — cohortative **لِـ** + first-person plural.

**مَكتَب** (maktab, office/desk) — **مَفعَل** place-noun from root **ك-ت-ب** (k-t-b, to write). Literally 'the place of writing.' Same root: **كِتَاب** (book), **كِتَابة** (writing), **كَاتِب** (writer), **مَكتُوب** (letter/written thing).
This root family beautifully shows how Arabic patterns relate: مَفعَل = place where the verb action happens.

**الهَوَاء الطَّلق** (al-hawāʾ aṭ-ṭalq) = the open air, literally 'the free/unrestrained air.' **هَوَاء** (hawāʾ, air) from root **ه-و-ي** (h-w-y, to blow/love). **طَلق** (ṭalq, free/open) from root **ط-ل-ق** (ṭ-l-q, to release/free). Note sun-letter assimilation: **الط** → *aṭ-ṭalq*.

**مُقَابَلة** (muqābala, interview/meeting) — Form III verbal noun from root **ق-ب-ل** (q-b-l, to face/accept). Form III (**فَاعَلَ**) implies two parties facing each other: **قَابَلَ** (qābala, to meet/face) → **مُقَابَلة** (meeting face to face). Related: **قَبل** (qabla, before), **قِبلة** (qibla, direction of prayer — the direction you face).
cards.englishPhrase: I have a job interview tomorrow.

cards.grammarNote:
**زَمِيل** (zamīl, colleague/fellow) from root **ز-م-ل** (z-m-l, to accompany/wrap). **فَعِيل** adjective pattern often means 'sharing a quality.' **زُمَلاء** (zumalāʾ) is the broken plural — pattern **فُعَلاء**, typical for **فَعِيل** adjectives: **صَدِيق → أَصدِقاء**, **زَمِيل → زُمَلاء**.
**وَدُودون** (wadūdūn, friendly) — **فَعُول** intensive pattern from root **و-د-د** (w-d-d, to love).

**عَمِيل** (ʿamīl, customer/agent/client) from root **ع-م-ل** (ʿ-m-l, work/do). The **فَعِيل** pattern can mean 'one who does' or 'one done to.' Here it denotes someone who works with you (a client). Plural: **عُمَلاء** (ʿumalāʾ — broken plural **فُعَلاء**).
Do not confuse with **عَامِل** (ʿāmil, worker) which uses the **فَاعِل** pattern.

**فَرِيق** (farīq, team/group) from root **ف-ر-ق** (f-r-q, to separate/distinguish). Literally 'a separated/distinct group.' **فَرَّقَ** (farraqa, to divide), **فَارَقَ** (fāraqa, to part from), **فُرقَة** (furqa, separation/troupe), **فَرِيق** also means a military brigade.
Plural: **فِرَق** (firaq — broken plural, pattern **فِعَل**).
cards.englishPhrase: I work in a great team.

cards.grammarNote:
**مَشرُوع** (mashrūʿ, project/enterprise/lawful) — passive participle **مَفعُول** from root **ش-ر-ع** (sh-r-ʿ, to begin/legislate). Literally 'something undertaken/begun.' Also means 'lawful/permissible' in religious law: **غَير مَشرُوع** = unlawful. Plural: **مَشَارِيع** (mashārīʿ — broken plural **مَفَاعِيل**).
**شِبه مُنتَهٍ** = almost finished — **شِبه** (shabah, semi-/almost) + indefinite active participle.

**اِجتِمَاع** (ijtimāʿ, meeting/gathering) — Form VIII verbal noun from root **ج-م-ع** (j-m-ʿ, to gather/collect). Form VIII (**اِفتَعَلَ**) often has a reflexive sense: **جَمَعَ** (jamaʿa, to gather/bring together) → **اِجتَمَعَ** (ijtamaʿa, to gather together/meet). **مُجتَمَع** (mujtamaʿ, society) is from the same root and form.
cards.englishPhrase: We have a meeting at ten.

cards.grammarNote:
**وَقت فَرَاغ** (waqt farāgh, free time) = time of emptiness/vacancy. **فَرَاغ** (farāgh, emptiness/free space) from root **ف-ر-غ** (f-r-gh, to empty). **وَقت** (waqt, time) + **فَرَاغ** = an **إضافة** (iḍāfa) possessive chain without the definite article on the first noun.
**مَاذَا تَفعَل؟** (mādhā tafʿal?) = What do you do? **مَاذَا** = what (used before a verb), while **مَا** is used before a noun.
cards.englishPhrase: What do you do in your free time?
